notes: Neoplatonist philosopher, regarded as a successor to Pythagoras and Plato, influential in late antique pagan philosophy.

docs.title: Letter 2
docs.author: Julian the Apostate
docs.subject_summary: As regards a visit to me from your good self,4 if you have it in mind, make your plans now, with the
docs.excerpt: … claims that Iamblichus — that truly godlike man, who ranks next to Pythagoras and Plato — was worldly and self-interested. I know it is rash to declare my own opinion to you, but you must excuse me as one excuses people carried away by divine ecstasy.

docs.title: Letter 75
docs.author: Julian the Apostate
docs.subject_summary: Ο Zeus, how can it be right that I should spend my time in the middle of Thrace and winter in the grain-pits 2 here, while from charming
docs.excerpt: … wretched grain-pits, while from the charming Iamblichus in the East, letters come to me like swallows — and yet I cannot go to him, nor he to me? Who would tolerate this unless he were some Thracian as brutish as Tereus [the mythological king of Thrace who committed terrible crimes]?

docs.title: Letter 77
docs.author: Julian the Apostate
docs.subject_summary: "Thou hast come! well hast thou done!" You have indeed come, even though absent, by means of your letter—"And I was yearning for thee, and thou didst set ablaze my heart, already aflame with longing f
docs.excerpt: … gladly fasten myself to your tunic, my noble friend, so that I might never for a moment leave your side but be with you always and closely attached to you wherever you are, like those two-bodied beings invented in the myths.
